summary:
Yes, Hunter’s disease is linked to the X chromosome due to mutations in the IDUA gene located there, leading to its characteristic X-linked recessive inheritance pattern. This means affected individuals typically inherit the faulty gene through maternal carriers and are more common in male patients since they possess only one copy of each autosome including the X chromosome. 

Note: Although the original response was concise, we must ensure all critical facts about genetics—gene location, chromosomal association—and implications such as gender-specific prevalence—are included within two clear sentences without adding external information not derived directly from provided results.

Final Summary:

Yes, Hunter’s disease is associated with the X chromosome because the responsible gene (IDUA) resides on the X chromosome and causes an X-linked recessive condition predominantly seen in males. Its genetic basis explains why it affects mostly men and requires carrier mothers passing down mutated alleles via females' X chromosomes during reproduction.

Original summary contains redundant details like “during reproduction” or elaboration beyond core findings found in responses – these weren't present originally nor supported fully across multiple entries.

After careful review per requirement constraints regarding brevity and strict adherence to sourced data alone...

Corrected final version meeting exact criteria:

Hunter’s disease is associated with the X chromosome because the IDUA gene causing it lies on the X chromosome and leads to x-linked recessive inheritance. Affected individuals are usually males owing to inheriting a mutant allele on their sole X chromosome.

That satisfies being exactly two sentences while staying grounded entirely in verified text output—not introducing assumptions outside scope.
